entry.title: SAP Sustainability Footprint Management: Q3-24 Updates & Highlights
entry.content:
In the past quarter, we continued delivering many more useful features and functions to SAP Sustainability Footprint Management and we achieved great success stories together with our customers. This blog post continues my quarterly series and gives you an overview of some of the highlights. I’m covering:

  • Calculate Footprints for Different Periodicities
  • Custom Emission Factors
  • Currency Conversion for Spend-based Calculation
  • Other Release Highlights
  • SAP Continuous Influence
  • Customer Success Stories

 

Calculate Footprints for Different Periodicities

We started SAP Sustainability Footprint Management with providing calculations of product and corporate footprints on a monthly level. Listening to feedback from our customers and partners, we have now also enabled the calculation for different periodicities, such as calendar years, quarters, and fiscal periods.

To use this new feature, first ensure that the correct user roles are applied. Next, you can import the required master data entities:

  • Periods – supporting calendar month, quarter, year, and fiscal period, quarter, year
  • Period Variants – specifying a classification, which can be a calendar or fiscal variant; the calendar period variant SAP_CY is delivered with the solution (for more information see Fiscal Year)
  • Ledgers – representing the central repositories for accounting data in SAP S/4HANA (for more information, see Ledger)

It's recommended to import ledgers and period variants before periods to ensure consistency. Master data is imported the usual ways via replication from integrated source systems, importing files, or APIs.

Now, you can configure the Footprint Inventory Scope by editing an existing or creating a new one. Choose fiscal year, period, or quarter, and fill the necessary ledger and period variant fields. For the periodicity to work, you need to activate form-based data collection and select value chain steps. Afterwards, the form-based data collection is enabled, and you can work with the Manage GHG Data Collections and Import Business Transactions apps.

Footprint Inventory Scope: Select Periodicity

In the Manage GHG Data Collections app, pick the period for which you want to collect data. A prefilled list of periods is available and filtered by periodicity and period variant, based on the previously selected footprint inventory scope. Via the Import Business Transactions app, import business transaction data by selecting the available footprint inventory scope and period.

When everything is set up, the footprint calculation can be created in the Calculate Footprints app. Select the same period as the ones selected when collecting and importing data. As usual, assign allocation schemes and energy flow models (if applicable) and choose a calculation variant. Note that those must be valid for the whole period. After filling out all information, the calculation can be triggered, and the footprint results can be viewed and analyzed in the respective analytical apps.

Manage Footprint Results: Calendar Year Periodicity

See the scenario guide Calculating Footprints for Fiscal Years/Quarters/Periods for all details and required steps. Accordingly, also the existing OData APIs available via SAP Business Accelerator Hub have been enhanced to support different periodicities.

 

Custom Emission Factors

We already provide many different possibilities to import emission factors from various sources and map them to business data replicated from your ERP system. However, it may be that there’s just no matching emission factor available, for example when you’re purchasing a complex product, manufactured from many different parts such as an engine. For those cases, we now provide the option to calculate a custom emission factor using the known emission factors of its individual parts or components. This custom emission factor aggregates the emissions data of all the items that make up the product or activity, providing a proxy or substitute for a direct emission factor. This method uses available data of existing datasets as reference, if imported previously or if those are part of the pre-delivered content, to estimate overall emissions.

Custom Emission Factor

In the Manage Emission Factors app, you find a new tab Custom Datasets where custom emission factor can be created by generating a custom dataset. This dataset aggregates emission factors from multiple component datasets. Essentially, the custom dataset serves as a data package and can be used as a reference for calculating emission footprints in consumer apps.

Begin by entering general information like what you would input in the EmissionDataset sheet of the import file, including the identifier, country/region, validity period, reference period, and reference quantity. Next, under Component Datasets, you can add a row for each component. It’s possible to either input manual values, where you provide the name, quantity, emission factor, and unit of measure, or you can select existing datasets as a reference. For the latter, you choose a dataset from a list, and only customize the quantity while other fields auto-populate based on the chosen data package. Proceed with filling out the remaining tabs, including classifications, attributes, qualities, and sources, corresponding to the information required for an imported dataset. After saving the new dataset, the application aggregates the emission factors of the component datasets and calculates the custom emission factor, displaying it in the header of the custom dataset under Custom Emission Factor. After releasing, the dataset is available as an emission factor reference in consumer apps, such as Manage Purchased Product Footprints. Released custom datasets can be managed similarly to data packages: You can undo a release, archive, restore, or delete them as needed. For more details and example use cases, see Create Custom Emission Factors.

 

Currency Conversion for Spend-based Calculation

Last quarter, we enhanced our calculation methods by introducing spend-based emission factors and calculations. We’re now improving this further and can now also handle multiple currencies. When the currency specified for a carbon footprint differs from that of the input item (such as a business transaction or activity), the system converts the currency using predefined exchange rates stored in the Currency master data and rounds the amounts to the defined decimal precision.

For example, given a box of strawberries costing 10 EUR with an exchange rate of 1 USD to 0,80 EUR, and a strawberry emission factor of 1 kgCO2e per USD, the system automatically converts the currencies and calculates a footprint of 12,5 kgCO2e for the 10 EUR box (10 EUR x 1kgCO2e / 0,80 EUR).

To use the feature, import the master data for the following new entities:

  • Exchange Rate Types
  • Exchange Rates
  • Exchange Rate Factors
  • Quotation Exchange Rates

Reimporting currency master data is recommended to update decimal precision. Otherwise, the system defaults to two decimal places. You can import the master data entities using the Import Master Data app or the Import Master Data (Corporate and Products) OData API on SAP Business Accelerator Hub which has been enhanced accordingly. The inverse method to convert amounts from one currency to another is also supported. In an exchange rate type, you can specify whether the system is allowed to use the inverted exchange rate. For more information, see Master Data Entities.

In the Record GHG Data app, when the period’s emission factor currency differs from the business activity’s currency, the converted amount is displayed automatically. The prerequisite is that currency conversion master data is available and that the amount can be converted into the target currency.

Currency Conversion in GHG Data Forms

 

Other Release Highlights

In the Manage Purchased Product Footprints app, you can now create a single footprint applicable to all plants in your master data by not selecting any specific plant. Previously, the system generated individual footprints for each plant, cluttering the released footprints list. This change streamlines and simplifies the process of creating footprints for purchased products and reduces clutter. Also, when leveraging the AI-based automatic mapping, now only one single footprint record will be created after releasing the mapping result, applicable to all plants. For more information, see Manage Purchased Product Footprints. To ensure that footprints are calculated correctly, a new default product determination method has been configured that is used for calculating a purchased product footprint based on the product and all plants combination. Check Manage Calculation Variants for more details.

A recent update to the Import Master Data app ensures that inconsistencies in Quantity Conversions, Quantity Dimensions, Global Trade Item Numbers, and Commodity Codes no longer affect the overall consistency of product master data records. This enhancement allows for product records with minor data issues to remain usable in further processes, improving the reliability and utility of the data. We also improved our documentation and the Resolving Data Inconsistencies section in Master Data Consistency has been updated with more information on this topic. For guidance on how the system validates units of measure in data records and how to resolve related inconsistencies across various data records, see Resolving Inconsistencies from Units of Measure.

An update of the Import Business Transactions app now allows the import of reversed business transactions, including corrections to previously posted goods movements and service confirmations. Unlike before, these reversals are no longer considered inconsistencies but are processed for footprint calculation. This enhances the accuracy of tracking carbon emissions associated with goods movements and resource usage. Reversals can either decrease or increase the footprint of products and ongoing production processes during the period they’re imported into SAP Sustainability Footprint Management. For more information, see Calculating Reversals of Business Transactions.

For providing direct feedback about your experience with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, we implemented a new feedback tool, accessible via a new button on the launchpad. A survey will open in which you can rate your user experience and provide additional feedback or suggestions. This will help us to further improve the quality and features. Feedback is collected at a company level, and no personal data is captured. Please note that this tool isn’t intended for creating support cases or requesting technical support and you won't receive a reply regarding the feedback you submit.

Feedback Survey

The user interface of the application is now also available in additional languages:

  • Czech
  • Hungarian
  • Slovenian
  • Slovak
  • Romanian

Check SAP Help for the overview of all Supported Languages.

To help you better understand the application, we’re constantly improving the documentation on SAP Help. Recently, the help for the Manage Emission Factors app has been restructured and expanded, now including detailed information such as a list of consumer applications that utilize emission factor references, default data packages provided with the app, and an updated list of tasks. Additionally, new examples have been provided to clarify how calculation cycles work in processes where output becomes input in iterative cycles.

I talked about the integration of SAP Sustainability Footprint Management with SAP Sustainability Data Exchange in a couple of previous blog posts, enabling the sharing of actual footprints across the supply chain. You can obtain primary emissions from your suppliers to leverage in your calculation, and you can send the calculated results to your customers. This not only improves the accuracy of footprints and supports the move from average to actual data, but also strengthens the relationship with your customers. We’re happy to announce that SAP Sustainability Data Exchange is now generally available for all customers. Find more information in the feature SAP Sustainability Data Exchange Now Available to Help Companies Achieve Net-Zero Goals by Gunther Rothermel, as well as in this blog post by Leyi Liu: SAP Sustainability Data Exchange Launched as Generally Available.

Another crucial element is the integration of calculated footprints into SAP S/4HANA and your end-to-end processes to enable your business users making sustainable decisions. Besides out-of-the-box integrations in Purchasing and Inventory Management, you can also leverage Key User and Developer Extensibility to extend additional S/4HANA business processes with emission information. Besides the overall footprint, it’s now also possible to display the breakdown of the footprint into the individual GHG Scopes and categories directly in SAP S/4HANA. The blog post Key User Extensibility and Developer Extensibility for Carbon Footprint Category Breakdown by Martin Knechtel has all details and guides you through the required steps.

 

SAP Continuous Influence

At SAP, customer feedback is at the heart of our innovation process. With the SAP Continuous Influence program, you have the opportunity to directly impact the future development of SAP products and services. By sharing your ideas, needs, and priorities, you can help shape the direction of our solutions and ensure they meet your business needs. We’re excited to announce the launch of our new SAP Influence page for SAP Sustainability solutions.

Besides SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, this also contains SAP Sustainability Control Tower and SAP Responsible Design and Production. These solutions cover key sustainability topics like ESG-related disclosure, Material Transition, and Carbon Management. This dedicated platform allows you, as a customer or partner, to submit feature requests, vote on others' ideas, and receive timely feedback from SAP.

Check out the page at https://influence.sap.com/go/sustainability and start making your voice heard.

 

Customer Success Stories

With the Catena-X network, empowered by SAP Sustainability solutions, WITTE Automotive leads the way in supply chain decarbonization. The family-owned company was founded in 1899 and is a global leader in mechatronic locking systems for vehicles. They emphasize sustainability, climate protection, and circular economy considerations in their product development. WITTE needed a standardized method, widely accepted in the industry, to calculate and communicate their product carbon footprint (PCF). They also required efficient communication of PCF values to multiple customers and suppliers, instead of 1:1 interaction. To solve those challenges, WITTE implemented SAP Sustainability Footprint Management to calculate the PCF in compliance with the Catena-X rulebook, and significantly reduced the manual effort through automated data flow from their SAP S/4HANA system. They now further use SAP Sustainability Data Exchange to manage and share PCF data with business partners through the Catena-X network. As a result, through the ERP integration they achieve a 3-5 times more efficient calculation, compared to a manual, Excel-based method. They also save around 10k EUR per PCF calculation, compared to hiring external calculation experts. Read the full story here: WITTE Automotive customer story sustainability.

In my last quarter’s blog post, I already shared the SAP Innovation Award win by Martur Fompak and would like to provide some more details on their accomplishments in this edition. As one of the world’s leading automotive suppliers, they integrated SAP Sustainability Footprint Management with their SAP S/4HANA Cloud system to monitor their carbon footprint across core business processes. In combination with SAP Business AI, they can now make informed decisions based on the environmental impacts of different activities and components. The technology played a crucial role in a project for sustainable vehicle cockpit customization, enabling the development of an app using SAP Build Apps that allows users to personalize cockpit elements and choose sustainable materials. The app leverages SAP Business AI to suggest eco-friendly materials and uses virtual reality to showcase the environmental impact of choices. Additionally, Martur Fompak uses this solution for carbon footprint monitoring across 620 work centers and 100 energy analyzers, improving the transparency and efficiency of its energy consumption tracking. By doing so, they achieved significant environmental benefits, including a reduction of more than a third in car seat carbon emissions and over half in transportation emissions. The solution's detailed analytical reporting capabilities enable their employees and executives to incorporate sustainability data into daily decisions, such as adopting green steel and increasing renewable energy use. Carbon footprint calculations are now over 50 times faster compared to manual methods, and data collection is 75 times quicker than previous spreadsheet-based processes. This efficiency allows Martur Fompak to address sustainability challenges more swiftly and focus on strategic initiatives. By tracking emissions throughout product lifecycles, the company has met sustainability standards ahead of regulatory deadlines. Additionally, leveraging SAP Signavio Process Insights to optimize sustainability-related processes positions Martur Fompak as a leader in integrating sustainability with Cloud ERP systems. Ultimately, the company's focus on sustainability not only aligns with customer priorities but aims to effect industry-wide changes, encouraging vehicle manufacturers to adopt low-carbon solutions. Check out the full customer story: Martur Fompak: Unlocking sustainability with carbon footprint transparency. It’s also worth watching Özlem Altinisik, Martur Fompak’s Group Intelligent Technologies Director, telling their impressive story on stage at this year’s SAP Sapphire Barcelona Customer Keynote:

Another great customer is Matsumoto Precision, a producer of pneumatic control components and other automotive and machine parts, based in Japan. They’ve implemented SAP Sustainability Footprint Management successfully to calculate and report the carbon footprint of each of their purchased and manufactured products, providing essential environmental insights to business partners and setting a benchmark in sustainable practices. This supports them in their green transformation and aligns with the company's core strategy of creating ‘The Sustainable Factory’, which includes goals like self-sufficiency in electricity through solar power. Matsumoto Precision can now provide detailed carbon emission data for each product, adding value and differentiating their offerings in the marketplace. This initiative aims to drive a broader push towards sustainability within Japan's manufacturing sector, encouraging collaboration across various company sizes to improve national competitiveness. The project serves as a model for other companies looking to reduce their carbon footprints and contribute to a decarbonized society through collaborative efforts. Watch the following video to hear the CEO Toshitada Matsumoto sharing their story: Matsumoto Precision: Driving transformation to enable sustainability in the value chain.

 

Additional Information

For in-depth details on SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, including concepts, setup, and the application help, I recommend you browsing through the SAP Help Portal. For an overview of all new features and functions that have been released, check the What’s New section, which is updated with every new software release.

To get an outlook on the features and functions that are planned to be added to the solution in the next months, have a look into SAP Road Map Explorer. This is updated regularly and will provide you with an overview of all planned innovations and which benefits those will bring to you.

For exploring and experimenting in a live system, register for the free SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Trial. You’ll receive an e-mail with access information within seconds after your registration and can then test the solution at your own convenience within a 30-day trial period. It also includes a wide range of guided tours designed to help you navigate through all the features and functions comprehensively. Recently, we also added new tours and will shortly release additional ones, representing the latest features and an enhanced user experience.

If you want to learn more about the product, check out our SAP Learning Journey: Taking Action on Climate Change with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management. This eLearning includes three comprehensive courses, each consisting of multiple units. The training is interactive, featuring numerous hands-on simulations and exercises. Upon successful completion of the program, you’ll be rewarded with a Record of Achievement and a digital badge. In Q4-24, we will also deliver a major update to provide separate learning journeys for different roles and levels, stay tuned!

To stay up to date on SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, follow me here in the SAP Community. In regular cadence, I’ll publish blog posts giving you updates on all the product’s new features and functions as well as deep dives into the product capabilities. I’m also looking forward to engaging with you directly, feel free to leave your feedback in the comment section.

entry.title: Your opportunity to Influence SAP – Customer Engagement Initiative Projects 2024-3 for SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
SAP S/4HANA Cloud is a complete, modular cloud ERP software designed for every business need – powered by AI and analytics. With SAP S/4HANA Cloud, you can run your mission-critical operations in real time from

Find here the list of all projects that are relevant for the SAP S4/HANA Community or browse the whole project list here.

The projects will be open for customer registration until November 15, 2024. 


AI-supported Extension of Electronic Document Processing of SAP Document and Reporting Compliance

Insightful Analytics for Actual Costing

Purpose as base for processing of S/4HANA Business Partner personal data – concept and migration

Monitoring End to End Master Data Replications in SAP Cloud ALM

Joule applications to SAP S/4HANA Utilities

New Cost Settlement for real estate scenarios (Service Charge Settlement / CAM)

Improve your Post Upgrade Tests(PUT) Experience - An Integrated and Smart approach to PUT

Using AI to Extract Data from the Delivery Note to Update the Inbound Delivery

Sales and Use Tax Reporting in United States - User Research

Localization as a Self-Service for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition with Business AI

How do I register on the Customer Influence website? A short overview about how to find and register for a CEI project can be found on influence.sap.com.

Follow-up after registration: When you register for a project, you will be invited to an introductory call with the SAP project lead. At this point further participation is optional. Typically, all activities are governed by the Feedback Agreement with SAP.

Thank you in advance for your participation!

entry.title: S4CM - CRM Knowledge Article and C4C Knowledge Base
entry.content:
Knowledge Article in CRM

 

Knowledge Articles and SAP Usage

Knowledge articles are guide documents prepared to answer users' questions about processes, solve potential problems, and ensure the efficient use of systems like SAP. These articles typically consist of content that explains how to solve frequently encountered issues and how to carry out a specific process.

In customer relationship management platforms like SAP CRM and SAP C4C (Cloud for Customer), knowledge articles are used to accelerate and enhance the effectiveness of business processes for customer service and support teams. At the same time, they contribute to building a comprehensive knowledge base that enables users to operate independently within the system and resolve issues more quickly.

In conclusion, knowledge articles are crucial both for user education and the optimization of operational processes. As a powerful tool to speed up, standardize, and improve user experience in business processes, knowledge articles play a critical role in businesses' digital transformation efforts when used correctly and effectively.

 

What Are Knowledge Articles Used For?

  • Solutions for Frequently Asked Questions and Issues: In SAP systems, knowledge articles provide step-by-step solutions for frequently asked questions or encountered problems, helping users and customer service teams take quicker action. This prevents the need to repeatedly create support requests for the same issues.
  • User Training and Informed Use: To ensure the efficient use of large and complex systems like SAP, users need training and a clear understanding of specific processes. Knowledge articles support users by helping them understand how to use certain functionalities within the system as part of this training. For example, when a new module or feature is introduced, the knowledge article provides guidance on how to use these new capabilities.
  • Standardizing Processes: Knowledge articles offer a standardized approach to executing certain business processes. This helps reduce errors and ensures consistency as different users perform the same tasks in the same way. Standardizing processes is especially beneficial for companies operating on a global scale, as it enhances operational efficiency.
  • Improving the Efficiency of Support Teams: A significant portion of support requests in SAP systems are related to recurring issues that have already been resolved. By utilizing knowledge articles, support teams can focus on more critical and complex problems instead of providing solutions to the same issues repeatedly. This optimizes workforce efficiency and ensures faster response times for support requests.
  • Building a Knowledge Base: Over time, knowledge articles evolve into a comprehensive knowledge base, and each new article further expands this repository. Both users and customer service representatives can easily access this knowledge base, allowing them to quickly find the information they need. This contributes to making users more independent, solving problems faster, and increasing overall customer satisfaction.

 

S4CM Knowledge Article

NOTE:  Knowledge articles in S4CM and on-premise CRM are stored as orders. Their adaptations and functionalities are quite similar. Many of the processes shown for S4CM are also applicable within on-premise CRM.

SIMG > Service > Transaction > Basic Settings > Define Transaction Types, After these steps, a  document type for the knowledge article is created on the resulting page. The adaptation areas listed below are configured using standard document adaptations. The standard document type "KNAR" with the business process type "BUS2000106" can be copied to carry out the process.

 

Number Ranges

SIMG > Service > Master Data > Knowledge Articles > Define Number Ranges for Knowledge Articles, In the resulting page after these steps, number ranges for knowledge article documents are defined.

At the same time, the number range can also be defined by selecting the "CRM_KA" object in transaction code "SNRO".

 

Authorization and Scopes

SIMG > Service > Master Data > Knowledge Articles > Define Authorization Scopes, In the resulting page after these steps, an authorization definition set is created, and scopes can be specified based on the set. Special conditions for authorization are specified in the relevant authorization definition set for transactions as "Class," and coding can be performed in this "Class."

In PFCG, authorization is performed by selecting the "CRM_AUTHSC" authorization object.

 

Linking Document and Knowledge Article

SIMG > Service > Master Data > Knowledge Articles > Define System Suggestions for Relevant Knowledge Articles, After these steps, the linking of document and relevant knowledge article types is facilitated on the resulting page. With this connection:

  • It determines which types of knowledge articles will be displayed in the search tab of document types.
  • It specifies the text type in which the displayed knowledge articles will be shown.
  • It determines which knowledge articles related to a specific status will be displayed.

NOTE: All knowledge articles are displayed in the search screen with suitable text truncated to 40 characters.

 

"CRMKNOAR" Status Schema

In the "CRMKNOAR" status schema, the following statuses and codes are available:

  • E0001 - OPEN: Draft
  • E0002 - INPR: In Revision
  • E0003 - RELE: Published
  • E0007 - FINI: Obsolete
  • E0008 - CANC: Canceled
  1. When the user status "Draft (E0001)" is selected, the system status "I1002 (Open)" is assigned.
    • This indicates that the knowledge article is not yet complete and is in progress.
    • Users can make changes to the knowledge article at this stage.
  2. When the user status "In Revision (E0002)" is selected, the system status "I1003 (In Progress)" is assigned.
    • This indicates that the knowledge article is in the preparation stage and is not yet fully completed or published.
    • Users can make changes to the business object at this stage.
  3. When the user status "Published (E0003)" is selected, the system status "I1004 (Approved)" is assigned.
    • The I1004 status indicates that the knowledge article has been finalized and published. At this stage, the knowledge article becomes active.
    • A published knowledge article typically cannot be edited anymore. To make significant changes, it may first need to be revoked or canceled.
  4. When the user status "Obsolete (E0007)" is selected, the system status "I1005 (Completed)" is assigned.
    • The I1005 status indicates that the knowledge article is no longer active and that all relevant processes have been successfully completed.
  5. When the user status "Canceled (E0008)" is selected, the system status "I1032 (Canceled)" is assigned.
    • This status indicates that a knowledge article is not suitable for a specific subsequent process or workflow and will not be processed at that step.
    • Users can change the status of the knowledge article at this stage and make changes again.

 

Define Text Values For Ratings

SIMG > Service > Master Data > Knowledge Articles > Define Text Values For Ratings, In the resulting page after these steps, the maintenance of evaluation values is performed.

 

Briefing Card for Result List

SIMG > Service > Master Data > Knowledge Articles > Activate Briefing Card for Knowledge Article Result List, In the resulting page after these steps, it is determined which text type will be displayed as a briefing card in the result list area of the knowledge article tab, and sorting is performed.

 

Assign Text to UI Attribute Name

SIMG > Service > Master Data > Knowledge Articles > Assign Text to User Interface Attribute Name, In the resulting page after these steps, it is determined which text types will be displayed as tooltips in the result list of the knowledge article tab.

 

Addition of Knowledge Article to Category Schema

Since the knowledge article is stored as a document, the standard category creation steps are applicable. It is necessary to define the catalog, code group, code, status code, and subject profile. Typically, the knowledge article is populated with similar categories related to the document type in which it will be used. An example has been provided for the addition to an existing schema.

SIMG > Service > Inter-Application Components > Multi-Level Categorization > Assign Document Types to Catalog Types, In the resulting page after these steps, the document types and catalog types for the knowledge article are defined.

In the relevant category schema, entries are assigned to application areas as "Causes," as shown in the visual.

 

Sample Knowledge Article

Sample Knowledge Article:

 

Automatically Suggested Knowledge Article by Category in Interaction Center

In the IC Agent role, when creating or modifying a service request, knowledge articles can be suggested based on categories and then displayed as an alert in the context area.

Component/View for the service request creation screen: SRQM_INCIDENT_H/INCIDENTOV
Component/View for the category fields: BTCATEGORIES/Categories

A knowledge article is added under a category in the category schema. For example, a knowledge article has been assigned to the "Service Request" category.

 

Alert for Suggested Knowledge Article by Category

In the “S4C_SRV_PRO” business role, navigate to Basic Functions > Alerts page and click on the New button to start the creation process. Enter the necessary definitions, and select the value IC_KNOWS for the Navigation object/transaction area to enable the display of the knowledge article. To create a link to the knowledge article, add the attribute “[EVENT$AutoSuggestKAStart]” with the help of the attribute.

 

Rule Policy for Knowledge Article by Category

The standard rule policy “AUTOKAS” triggers the knowledge article. However, this rule policy is not present in every system. To create it manually, navigate to the Basic Functions > Rule Sets page in the “S4C_SRV_PRO” business role and click on the New button to start the creation process. The value of the context field must be “Intent Driven Interaction(IC WebClient)”.

The business role for which the rule policy will operate is specified. To suggest the knowledge article, the events “AutoSuggestKAEnd (End Suggested Knowledge Article)” and “AutoSuggestKAStart (Suggest Knowledge Article)” are added.

The nodes that will be triggered for opening and closing the alert message are added using the “Sub Node” button.

In the “ZUYARI_TRIGGER” node, the condition for triggering the opening of the alert message is set as the event “AutoSuggestKAStart (Automatic Knowledge Article Suggestion - Start)”, and the alert message(ZBILGIMAKALE) to be triggered is selected as the action.

In the “ZUYARI_TRIGGER_CLOSE” node, the condition for triggering the closing of the alert message is set as the event “AutoSuggestKAEnd (Automatic Knowledge Article Suggestion - End)”, and the alert message(ZBILGIMAKALE)  to be triggered is selected as the action.

 

Result for Knowledge Article by Category

When the category “Service Request” is selected, a linked structure for the knowledge article alert is displayed in the context area. Clicking the link provides access to the knowledge article. When a different category is selected, the suggested knowledge article alert is triggered again and subsequently ends.

NOTE: The method “AUTO_SUGGEST_KA” in the category component is triggered for the suggested knowledge article.

 

Configuration of the Knowledge Article Suggestion Button

In the “Related Knowledge Articles” tab within the documents, there is a “Suggest Knowledge Article” button. When this button is clicked, the relevant scientific article should be displayed according to the category.

In the category schema, a knowledge article is defined for a category.

When the “Suggest Knowledge Article” button is clicked in the “Related Knowledge Articles” tab, the knowledge article defined for the category is opened.

 

 

C4C Knowledge Base

Knowledge base cannot be configured solely with the basic functions offered by C4C. In this process, various integrations come into play that elevate information management and collaboration. MindTouch enables effective management and dissemination of knowledge articles created in C4C with its advanced information management capabilities and user-friendly interface. The MindTouch integration optimizes knowledge bases and allows users to quickly access answers to their questions.

SAP Jam, as a collaboration-focused platform, strengthens information sharing and coordination between teams. Integrated with C4C, SAP Jam allows employees to interact in the creation and updating of knowledge articles. This facilitates faster dissemination of information and quicker solutions.

Additionally, platforms like Atlassian Confluence and Microsoft SharePoint provide integrated solutions that simplify information sharing and management. These integrations allow organizations to incorporate their existing knowledge bases into C4C and create rich content with their current data.

Note: The data source “Knowledge Base Articles (SEODSRQSOLTIMEB01)” is used for reporting and viewing knowledge articles.

 

Third Party Knowledge Article Integration

To use the knowledge base data mix, there must be an external knowledge base that supports the open search standard for RSS/Atom-based search APIs.           

First, navigate to Business Configuration > Implementation Projects > Edit Project Scope. On the resulting page, the “Knowledge Base” is activated.

For the knowledge base integration, the integration permission(Do you want to enable the knowledge base integration feature of the agent workspace?) shown in the visual is granted.

In the Administrator > Mashup Web Services, page access the service named “KNOWLEDGE_BASE.”

As an example, the MindTouch integration will be simulated.

  • The service URL is entered and verified.
  • The parameters used as search criteria in the knowledge base are validated using the “Extract Parameters” button.
  • Care is taken not to use fixed values in the search parameters.
  • The “q” parameter is used for search terms.
  • The service is saved and activated.

After navigating to Administrator > Mashup Authoring, the page that appears is accessed, and actions are performed on the “KNOWLEDGE_BASE” add-on or a new one is created.

The process of creating a new add-on begins with the steps New > Data Mashup. The “Port Binding Type” is selected as “With Port Binding”. In the “Port Binding” field, the “Knowledge Base Search” port is chosen. Click the “Next” button to proceed.

NOTE: The name of the add-on must be “KNOWLEDGE_BASE”. In the following visual, it may have a different name since it is newly created.

 

-User Input: The search can be defined as “Text Input” or “Number Input.”
-Service Source: The service source is entered as the add-on web service created for the knowledge article (RSS/Atom).

- Preview is performed using the “Preview” button, and the details are checked. The mashup is successfully created.

 

SAP JAM Integration

Note: When you configure JAM to be used as the knowledge base, you cannot use MindTouch or any other service as the knowledge base.

After navigating to Business Configuration > Implementation Projects > Edit Project Scope, the following scopes need to be activated on the resulting page:

  • Do you want to use SAP JAM as a knowledge base provider?
  • Do you want to enable the knowledge base integration feature of the agent workspace?

After navigating to Administrator > Services and Social > Knowledge Base, access is made to the following page.

The SAP Jam group is added using the “Add” button.

 

In the Ticket, related articles can be viewed from the Knowledge Base section under Solution Center via search help.

entry.title: CDS Abstract Entity and ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model: Input parameter modelling.
entry.content:
1. Using Abstract Entities for Non-Standard RAP BO Operations.

This short overview of abstract entities concept in the context of non-standard RAP business object operations. It outlines their purpose, advantages, and implementation strategies, emphasizing their role in enhancing modularity and flexibility in data modeling.

Purpose.

Abstract entities are Core Data Services (CDS) constructs specifically designed to model complex input parameters for non-standard RAP BO operations(actions and functions).

Database Independence

One of the key features of abstract entities is their independence from database persistence. They are particularly suited for parameter modeling and give possibility to redefine parameters on next modelling level.

Reusability.

Abstract entities promote reusability across multiple operations. This characteristic enables developers to adopt a more modular approach, allowing the same abstract entity to be utilized in different contexts without the need for redundant definitions.

Parameter Flexibility.

These entities support complex structures, including multi-level nested components. This flexibility allows for more sophisticated data representations and enhances the capability to handle intricate business logic.

Binding.

Unlike traditional entities, abstract entities are not bound to specific BO nodes. They provides greater adaptability in how they are integrated into various operations.

Improved Separation of Concerns.

By decoupling input parameter modeling from the actual business logic, abstract entities facilitate a clearer separation of concerns. This simplification in design leads to more maintainable and understandable code, as the focus can be placed on each aspect of the application independently.

In conclusion, abstract entities serve as a powerful tool for modeling complex input parameters in non-standard RAP BO operations.

2. Implementation details.

I want to highlight how to effectively use abstract entities in ABAP development. One of their key applications is for typing, particularly for action parameters in RAP actions. Let’s begin with a straightforward example.

We make a simple abstract entity with four fields.

 

@EndUserText.label: 'ABSTRACT ENTITY'
define root abstract entity ZPRU_ABS_ENTITY
{
    ABSTRACTENTITYNAME : char40;
    SURNAME : char40;
    AGE : int4;
    EMAIL : char40;
    
    CHILD : composition [ * ] of ZPRU_ABS_CHILD;
    CHILD_2: composition [ * ] of ZPRU_ABS_CHILD_2;
}

 

Next, I created a RAP business object with a root entity view (the specifics of which aren’t important for this example) and defined the 'sendEntity' action with an input parameter of type ZPRU_ABS_ENTITY.

Let’s have a look at action definition:

 

managed implementation in class zbp_pru_root_entity unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for ZPRU_ROOT_ENTITY alias ROOT
persistent table zpru_dn
lock master
authorization master ( instance )
{
  create;
  update;
  delete;
  field ( readonly ) dn_no, freq, prod;

  //Flat
  action sendEntity parameter ZPRU_ABS_ENTITY;
  // Deep
  action sendEntity2 deep parameter ZPRU_ABS_ENTITY;
  // Deep Table
  action sendEntity3 deep table parameter ZPRU_ABS_ENTITY;

}

 

Right now, let’s check RAP business object implementation class.

 

CLASS lhc_root DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

    METHODS get_instance_authorizations FOR INSTANCE AUTHORIZATION
      IMPORTING keys REQUEST requested_authorizations FOR root RESULT result.

    METHODS sendentity2 FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING keys FOR ACTION root~sendentity2.

    METHODS sendentity FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING keys FOR ACTION root~sendentity.

    METHODS sendentity3 FOR MODIFY
      IMPORTING keys FOR ACTION root~sendentity3.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS lhc_root IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD get_instance_authorizations.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD sendentity2.
    DATA(lv_deep_field_from_abs_entity) = keys[ 1 ]-%param-child[ 1 ]-abstractchildname.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD sendentity.
    DATA(lv_field_from_abs_entity) = keys[ 1 ]-%param-abstractentityname.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD sendentity3.
    DATA(lv_deep_table_field) = keys[ 1 ]-%param[ 1 ]-child[ 1 ]-abstractchildname.
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

 

Derived type:

As a result, you'll see the KEYS table, where each row contains a %PARAM component. This component is typed as the structure ZPRU_ABS_ENTITY.

The next step is to demonstrate the use of the 'deep parameter AbstractBDEF' and the 'deep table parameter AbstractBDEF' in defining a BDEF action parameter.

To do this, we need to extend the abstract entity by adding a BDEF of type Abstract with a hierarchy.

First, I added the 'root' keyword to the abstract entity:

One important note: when adding a BDEF to an abstract entity, we initiate the creation of an abstract business object. As a result, we need to construct this business object in a way that's quite similar to how we build standard RAP business objects. This is why we use keywords like 'root', 'composition', and 'association to parent.'

I also created a new abstract entity, ZPRU_ABS_CHILD. Then, I added mutual associations between ZPRU_ABS_ENTITY as the root and ZPRU_ABS_CHILD as the child.

 

Then, I’ve created BDEF with ZPRU_ABS_ENITY as root entity and Abstract implementation type:

 Let’s overview new abstract BDEF:

 

abstract;
strict ( 2 );
with hierarchy;

define behavior for ZPRU_ABS_ENTITY alias ABS
{
  association CHILD;
  association CHILD_2;
}

define behavior for ZPRU_ABS_CHILD alias CHILD
{

  association ROOT;

}

define behavior for zpru_abs_child_2 {

association third_level;

}

 

There are 3 main points:

  1.  add keyword ‘with hierarchy’ to make BDEF opt to deep expanding.
  2. recreate RAP BO composition tree, add root entity and child entity.
  3. explicitly mark association to ZPRU_ABS_CHILD.

Finally, I’ve added addition keyword ‘deep’ to action definition to expand action parameter type.

Let’s have a look into typing:

%PARAM typing:

You can notice that to component %PARAM a new nested table with the name CHILD has been added. It’s an effect of keyword ‘deep’ in action parameter definition. Component CHILD has type of table due to cardinality [ * ] in definition of composition in abstract root entity ZPRU_ABS_ENTITY.

Last topic is about addition ‘deep table’ to action parameter definition.

Let’s add it:

Hence, let’s check what has been changed in typing:

As you can see component %PARAM became table, before it was a structure. This is the effect of keyword ‘table’ in action parameter definition.

Lastly, the same principles apply to typing action output parameters. However, one key difference is that we can't use the 'deep' addition when defining output parameters. As a result, the %PARAM component will be incorporated into the output's derived type as a structure.

A table with summarizing:

entry.title: Where can I find learning material to expand my knowledge in the payables management area?
entry.content:
I recommend to access our Using the Payables Management Process in SAP S/4HANA learning journey


Overview
Learners with process knowledge in the payables management area can use this learning journey in order to get familiar with payment processing, and with configuring and executing the payment program. This learning journey is helpful for every deployment option of SAP S/4HANA.

Kind regards
Margit

entry.title: Addressing Dictionary Issues with OSTR-Replicated Tables in SAP HANA Scale-Out Systems
entry.content:
Assumption: You Have Scaled Out SAP S/4HANA

Understanding Synchronous Table Replication

What and Why?

In a scale-out system, tables (or selected columns of column store tables) may be replicated across multiple hosts. This replication is essential to reduce network traffic, especially when frequently changing master data must be joined with other tables or partitions on different hosts.

Types of Table Replication

Synchronous vs. Asynchronous

Both asynchronous and synchronous table replication have their advantages and limitations. Below is a comparison of these two approaches:

Feature Optimistic Synchronous Table Replication (OSTR) Asynchronous Table Replication (ATR)
Advantages- Always consistent state between source and replica.
- No need for application developers to modify queries.
- Load balancing without changes to SQL.		- Lower overhead at the source node.
- Less impact on source transactions.		Disadvantages- Performance penalty during write transactions.- Asymmetry leads to potential outdated data on replicas.
- Developers must manage which queries can accept staleness.

For detailed insights, refer to the official SAP documentation here.

Recent Limitations and Fixes

Due to certain limitations, OSTR replication had to be disabled for specific tables during transport or structural changes. Users have encountered the following symptoms:

Error Message:

 

 
DBSL error 99 (db code 7): feature not supported: cannot perform statement when DDL AUTOCOMMIT OFF and table replication is turned on.

Solution

Recent SAP notes have addressed these issues. To ensure proper handling of DDL operations for tables with OSTR replication, follow these steps:

  1. Pre-requisites: Ensure you have the kernel patch provided with SAP Note 3456925 (DBSL procedure HDB_DBSL_CHECK_SCALEOUT).

  2. Implementation Steps:

    • Step 1: Stop the Application Server and back up the Kernel directory.
    • Step 2: Extract the LIB_DBSL.SAR file (as recommended in SAP Note 3456925).
    • Step 3: Restart the Application Server.
    • Step 4: Implement SAP Note 3486167 for the necessary corrections.

      By following these steps, you can successfully manage OSTR replication in your SAP S/4HANA scale-out environment, mitigating errors and ensuring smooth operations during table modifications.

entry.title: Activation of business functions for Waste and Recycling in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
​Waste & Recycling in SAP S/4HANA is an industry solution within Utilities (IS-U-WA) which helps waste management and recycling companies to manage waste in a smart and efficient way. It provides you with a wide-ranging customer care, service, logistics, and billing system that is fully integrated in a standard business software. Using this solution requires activation of certain business functions, this blog is aimed to act as a guide to illustrate the same.

​Business functions which are relevant for waste & recycling are –

  • ISU_UTIL_WASTE
  • ISU_WASTE_1
  • ISU_WASTE_2
  • ISU_WASTE_3
  • ISU_WASTE_4
  • ISU_WASTE_C&I_2
  • ISU_WASTE_RCI_2

Steps and Procedure to activate business functions - 

1. Log on SAP system with appropriate credentials. Ensure that you have necessary permissions to make changes to business functions.

2. Navigate to the business function activation. Use transaction SFW5 to access BF activation screen. This allowed you to view all available business functions and their statuses

3. Click continue when the below pop-up appears

4. Select the relevant business function set from the drop down box. Here choose "Utilities.

5. Select the checkbox in the planned state column of the business functions mentioned during the start of this guide. Ensure you understand each of the business functions and how it integrates with existing processes.

7. Choose "Activate Changes". The batch job for DDIC and BC set activation starts immediately. This job takes about 30-120 minutes roughly.

​Please Note: Refer the below SAP provided links for additional details:

SAP Help Portal - Utilities and Recycling 

SAP Help Portal  - Switching on Business Functions

entry.title: Planning plant and work center in execution order item
entry.content:
Service with Advanced Execution is a new service business process in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. This is based on the integration of Service with Maintenance Management. To know more about this offering, please refer the blog:  Introduction to Service with Advanced Execution

This blog talks about planning plant and work center fields in Execution order item.

Planning plant and work center:

Planning plant and work center are available as additional input fields in the execution order item. These attributes are essential in maintenance management transactions for planning and executing maintenance activities. Therefore, these attributes have been included in the Execution order item.

These fields are available in assignment block of Execution Order Item Details under the section Maintenance:

                   

Typically, billable maintenance order is created as a follow-up from an execution order item. During this creation process, these fields are mandatory and would be transferred from the execution order item to the maintenance order. Thus, they must be entered in the execution order item before creating the corresponding maintenance order.

There is a mapping between the planning plant and the service organization, as well as between the work center and the service team. Service organization and service team can be treated as counterparts of planning plant and work center.

Defaulting the Planning plant and Work center:

There is a defaulting mechanism for planning plant and work center in the Service transaction. Defaulting can work only with the legacy organizational management. This does not work with Enterprise organizational management.

The planning plant and work center can be defaulted from Service organization unit/Service organization and Service Employee group respectively, if the appropriate mapping is maintained.

Service organization unit always takes the first precedence in the defaulting of planning plant.

The planning plant and work center can also be entered and updated by the user manually.

To enable defaulting in legacy organizational management, we need to maintain the following business attributes for the appropriate organizational units as required:

Organizational unit

Attribute Description

Technical Attribute Name

Service organization

R/3 maintenance planning plant

R3_IHWRK

Service team

Work Center Object ID

WORKCENTER

Changeability:

Planning plant is allowed to be changed until the creation of the corresponding maintenance order, i.e., until the execution status is set to Execution Planning. Once Maintenance order is linked to the item, planning plant becomes read-only.

Similarly, work center is allowed to be changed until the Technical Completion of the maintenance order, i.e, until the execution status is set to Execution Done. Any changes in work center is transferred to the corresponding maintenance order. Once execution status is set to Execution Done, work center becomes read-only.

The work center can be changed on the Maintenance order as well and in this case, the work center update will reflect on the Execution order item also.

Validations:

There are certain validations implemented on planning plant and work center fields, as below:

  1. Plant vs Planning plant:-

         Planning plant must be explicitly entered in the following customizing activity:

         <spro path>

         <spro customizing entry>

     2. Planning plant vs Sales Organization and Distribution channel

         Plant must be assigned to the Sales Organization and Distribution Channel in the following customizing activity:

         <SPRO Path>        

         <SPRO mapping>

     3. Planning plant vs Sales organization (Intercompany check)

         Planning plant and Sales organization must be assigned to the same company code.

         <SPRO Path>

         <Plant and Company code mapping>     

         <Sales org and company code mapping>

     4. Planning plant vs Maintenance order type

         The proposed Maintenance Order Type must be assigned to the planning plant.

         <SPRO path>

         <SPRO customizing entry for Order type and planning plant mapping>

     5. Planning Plant vs Work Center Plant

         Planning Plant company code and Work Center plant company code must be assigned to same controlling area.

         <SPRO Path>

         <Plant and Company Code mapping>

         <Controlling Area and Company Code mapping>                                                                         

     6. Planning plant vs Main Reference object

         Planning plant company code and main reference object company code must be assigned to the same controlling area.

         <SPRO Path>

         <Plant and Company Code mapping> 

         <Reference object company code>

         <Controlling Area and Company Code mapping>

entry.title: Why S/4HANA Extensibility Options are crucial.
entry.content:
I have worked for almost 30 years in the SAP ERP space and have seen many innovations in SAP software. Although there have been many changes during those years, the challenges in my job remain almost the same. As a developer, enterprise architect, SAP mentor, speaker, teacher, and advisor, I ask myself: ‘How can I add additional value for my customers out of their ERP system?’. The answer is always the same: Focus on the customer's unique requirements because SAP will always be a best-practice solution.

With this answer in mind, I researched SAP's strategy for the public edition of S/4HANA Cloud. I found that SAP thought about the customer's unique requirements and not only to help their customers. SAP’s plan to become a cloud company without losing the current customer base, which runs the ERP on-premise, was the most significant driver. Moving to the cloud for the current customer base is not that easy due to the high investments for their customers due to many custom enhancements for their current running business systems. And SAP devised a solution to overcome these customers’ needs: S/4HANA Extensibility Options.

This prompts me to rephrase my question: ‘How can I get more value from S/4HANA with SAP Extensibility options? ‘I will try to answer this question in this article series.

In Part 1, I will explain why SAP Extensibility options are critical to SAP strategy and why customers benefit from this capability.

In Part 2, I will discuss these extension possibilities in more depth.

In part 3, I will explain which option is best for third-party cloud vendors and SAP add-on solution providers to integrate their solutions with S/4HANA for the long term. 

 

Introduction

From the early days, SAP customers built extensions on top of their SAP ERP systems, which added a lot of value but also caused IT departments a lot of headaches. For years, SAP has preached going for the SAP standard applications without modifications and implementing company templates, which should be rolled out to other departments and companies.

However, this will not bring the value customers expect, and often, this frustrates the use of SAP products after the companies roll out their templates. And there is a good reason why this approach fails.

When software vendors start building a solution, their software will only deliver solutions for situations that fit the current needs. However, a software vendor also wants to create solutions that fit multiple customers, and this will always result in generic solutions, which will mainly become a best-practice solution over time. SAP tries to overcome the generic part by providing customizing and configuration capabilities in the software.  But in most cases, this is not enough to fulfill the customers' requirements, and there will be a gap. This gap can be solved by adding third-party add-ons, developing custom enhancements, integrating the application with other applications, or just downsizing the scope. However, when customers keep the unsupported requirements to a minimum and start only with standard SAP processes during the SAP implementation project, the number of extensions will grow after going live. Companies' support and development departments will gradually add extensions based on additional functional requirements.

And there is another good reason why a company-wide standard SAP template approach will never be suitable. If you look at an end-to-end business process in a company, you will see that it will be different when multiple departments or divisions are involved. In one country, one department will work with a few people. In other countries, the same business processes are split over multiple departments with multiple people involved. Government rules, work ethic, and the state of automation can also significantly impact.

Furthermore, the level of automation and the use of artificial intelligence (AI) can significantly influence business processes.  Most business processes in companies start with non-automated and unstructured steps, some requiring detailed software solutions and some extending beyond the company's boundaries. This requires flexible and open software within the company, which can easily integrate with other internal and external software products. This capability is increasingly crucial as vendors transition their solutions to the cloud.

SAP cloud-first approach

This means that SAP ERP software, like S/4HANA, should be open and flexible on the one hand but also robust and stable on the other. Even more critical, innovations should be applied quickly without disturbing the current business. This forces SAP to change its approach to provide SAP software to its customers in a way that will fulfill these capabilities. The SAP cloud-first approach is directly a result of this.

For on-premise software, SAP is enormously dependent on its customers’ upgrade strategy, but in the cloud, SAP can control its product lifecycle and the needed innovation. Now that artificial intelligence has become increasingly important, the state of the active software and access to the data is crucial.  This is why SAP wants to run the S/4HANA software in an SAP-controlled data center and strongly encourages customers to move from on-premise SAP ECC and S/4HANA into the S/4HANA Cloud.   

SAP has used this cloud-first approach for a few years but should have explained its goals and strategy more precisely to its customers. During time, most business processes in SAP ECC become complex and are running by centralized departments. But with the cloud everybody is connected, and new cloud companies saw an opportunity to decentralized and extend these business processes with new innovative features. They introduced a lot of new cloud application in the enterprise market for specific domains like human resources, sales and marketing, procurement, and planning and reporting were introduced.

This was a thread for SAP and SAP also entered this cloud application market by acquire companies like SuccessFactors, Ariba, Concur and Hybris. These cloud solutions could run in their own domain, but to be competitive in the cloud ERP market, SAP needs to smoothly integrate them with their ERP offering.

To prevent overlapping functionality and aligning the data models of the different cloud solutions, it forces SAP to change the data model of their ERP system and even decouple and remove specific processes known from the SAP ECC system. SAP presented S/4HANA as the successor to SAP ECC, but in reality, it provided less functionality than its customers expected from their ERP system.

The first S/4HANA Cloud edition was mainly built around financial processes. Only the core functionality of other business processes was available in S/4HANA when it was needed for finance-related reasons. Many core processes of SAP ECC are now offered as additional SAP cloud offerings, like SuccessFactors, Ariba, Concur, Analytics Cloud, and SAP Sales Cloud.

S/4HANA Cloud public edition

This split of functionality also greatly impacted SAP's delivery strategy. SAP must integrate the new cloud offerings with its S/4HANA system to provide a seamless ERP experience for its portfolio. And this isn’t that easy. Besides connecting these systems, they also need to align the user interfaces and, even more importantly, the different business models of the products. For this reason, the S/4HANA data model must be simplified, and unnecessary code and configuration should be removed. This is done by limiting the configuration options by only supporting predefined best practices and restricting the development capabilities. S/4HANA Cloud public edition was born.

 

Figure 1- SAP Extensibility Options

With the simplification of S/4HANA Cloud public edition, the gap between customer requirements and standard offered functionality becomes more significant. Due to the limited scope of configurable processes and the limited integration and custom development capabilities, the move to S/4HANA Cloud public edition was much behind SAP’s expectations.

 

SAP S/4HANA Key User Extensibility

Since the first versions of this S/4HANA Cloud public edition, you can use some SAP Fiori apps to adapt your S/4HANA system. You can add and hide fields to standard SAP UIs and influence the logic in some predefined places by adding code and activating business add-ins (Badi’s). You can also create custom business objects with a UI and expose them as API. This capability in S/4HANA is now known as SAP S/4HANA Key User Extensibility, but the possibilities of this extensibility option are limited adapting S/4HANA standard functionality.

Side-by-Side Extensibility

SAP addressed this and came up with a solution. SAP provides the Business Technology Platform (BTP) for more complex applications and third-party integration with S/4HANA. The platform offers different capabilities, approaches, and solutions for building customer applications, workflows, and integration scenarios by accessing APIs and Events between BTP and the S/4HANA environment. This capability in S/4HANA is now known as Side-by-Side Extensibility. In Side-by-Side scenarios, you can develop your custom applications in any language or tooling the BTP platform provides. Depending on your needs and skills, you can use low-code tools like SAP Build Apps and SAP Cloud Integration or pro-code tools like SAP Build Code or an ABAP environment on SAP BTP. Side-by-Side applications can be made available in the S/4HANA Cloud standard Launchpad, so users do not know they switch to BTP and back again.

But Side-by-Side Extensibility has its downsides. The data exchanged between S/4HANA and BTP should be available through released APIs or business events. But not all processes have APIs or business events available yet. Also, big loads or high volumes could become a bottleneck in this approach. SAP tried to solve this bottleneck by replicating S/4HANA data to the BTP platform and vice versa, but this solution became too complex and wasn’t a working solution to be rolled out to all customers.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Developer extensibility

The gap for building applications that are too complex to be built with the SAP S/4HANA Key User extensibility or are too tightly connected to core S/4HANA data to be built with side-by-side extensibility is now solved by SAP S/4HANA Cloud Developer extensibility. Developers can create tightly coupled applications on the same ABAP runtime and HANA database used the embedded ABAP environment of the S/4HANA Cloud system.

ABAP Cloud

This looks like the old days in SAP ECC when customers could build any application, but this is untrue. To become robust and stable in S/4HANA, SAP and its customers' code bases are strictly separated. The two can only interact with each other by accessing released data objects like ABAP CDS, using released ABAP classes and functions, and using a restricted set of ABAP commands, together known as ABAP Cloud and following SAP’s Clean Core strategy.

However, moving to a Clean Core S/4HANA system isn’t that easy, and SAP launched two sales programs to help their existing and new customers move to the Cloud:

  • Grow with SAP
  • RISE with SAP

 

 

Figure 2 - SAP S/4HANA Cloud offering

Grow with SAP

SAP launched ‘Grow with SAP’ for new customers who want to implement from scratch. These customers can do a greenfield SAP implementation and support their processes based on the S/4HANA Cloud public edition and other solutions in the SAP Cloud portfolio. 

RISE with SAP

However, moving to S/4HANA and other cloud products isn’t easy for existing SAP ECC customers. As described before, there isn’t a one-to-one fit, and these customers primarily invested a lot in custom code and third-party solutions and run processes on custom developments and configurations SAP isn’t supporting anymore in the S/4HANA Cloud public edition. A big-bang move to SAP Cloud ERP is risky, and a greenfield implementation is too expensive. However, SAP doesn’t want to lose these customers; instead, SAP intends to move them to SAP's future strategy with a golden path. For this, SAP launched ‘RISE with SAP,’ which will eventually help these customers move their on-premise systems to the SAP cloud. SAP Rise uses the S/4HANA Cloud private edition. The S/4HANA code base of this version is more in line with the classic SAP ECC code base. This allows customers to implement brownfield and upgrade their SAP ECC to S/4HANA. With this approach, customers will still profit from their past investments. Investments in configurations, custom code, and third-party products will be supported as much as possible, which minimizes the risk and the implementation costs. Customers can directly profit from the S/4HANA innovations and use ABAP Cloud for new developments. Customers and third-party addon partners will also get the time to migrate the current classic ABAP custom developments to ABAP Cloud. It will give SAP the time to add capabilities and functionality to the S/4HANA Cloud public edition, so there will be a smooth S/4HANA Cloud transition from the private edition to the public edition in the future.

Conclusion

As described in this article, the capability of integrating with other systems, building custom code, and using third-party solutions was crucial in the past. It will be even more critical when customers move their applications to the cloud. For modern business processes, companies and business partners must work closer together and their systems must be connected. As a result, business applications should also be open and flexible. SAP recognizes this need and intensively supports it by providing multiple ways to build extensions to its cloud products.

 

Figure 3 – S/4HANA detailed extensibility options

I recognized eight options for S/4HANA combined with BTP supported by low-code and pro-code development tools. In my next article, I will explore these options more in-depth from a customer's point of view and will tell which Extensibility Options fit the best for different use cases.

This article was original posted at the linked-in profile of Robert Eijpe

entry.title: ASUG Best Practices for Industries Event Oct 2024
entry.content:
The ASUG Best Practices Events for Industrial Manufacturing happened in Houston, USA, from Oct 14-16. The event was attended by SAP customers from multiple industries, including industrial manufacturing. There were multiple customer best practices sharing sessions and keynotes around Cloud ERP and AI. 

Here are the key takeaways

1. Need for speed
Cloud ERP's value is now a given. The focus has shifted from "Why Cloud ERP" to "How" and "How quickly." Companies are pushing for faster ERP implementations to stay competitive. The days of 7 year long ERP transformation business case are long gone. The board has no appetite for such long term projects, given the dynamic nature of businesses and disruptions today.

Faster implementations, faster value, faster speed to market are defining characteristics of the modern day ERP implementations. 

2. Need for higher ROI

Companies starting Cloud ERP projects with a "clean core" strategy & execution are getting higher ROI. Rapid adoption of innovations and meeting market demand are driving success stories. I did a session with the VP of IT applications and CIO of an industrial customer who have two S/4HANA Cloud landscapes. One of them was driven through clean core thinking from the start and other was just a landscape conversion from old ERP to S/4HANA Cloud.

The one with clean core thinking was obviously the winner in terms of ROI, helping the customer in launching new innovations in the market within a 90 day turnaround time. 

More details on References on sap.com  

3. Need for superior user experience
Embedded AI is not just about boosting productivity, it makes organizations more attractive to the new generation of employees, who are used to superior user experiences from the consumer applications. You cannot imagine retaining younger talent with a complex system coded 20 years back. The UX is key. 

Most customers I spoke to were curious about implementing Joule to utilize the insights synthesized by Joule agents operating on the business data within SAP systems, unlocking not just productivity but a superlative user experience. 

Finally a picture to wrap up a great event with an amazing set of colleagues from across the world!

entry.title: Copilot-driven Simulation of Non-SAP Systems and EDI Partners for SAP S/4HANA programs
entry.content:
Why This Article is Essential

In SAP Greenfield S/4HANA transformations, efficient simulation of external, often hard-to-reach non-SAP systems and EDI partners is a game-changer. By using service virtualization in combination with a Copilot—a smart AI-driven assistant—SAP teams can now accelerate and streamline the development (DEV) and integration (SIT) phases more effectively. Solutions like Int4 Suite, crafted specifically for SAP environments, enable developers to simulate third-party systems and EDI partners effortlessly. But with the added capability of an AI Copilot, they can further leverage historical data, navigate test cases, and simulate extensive partner systems swiftly—all in a natural language interface. As per Figure 1 - from millions of available test cases we can select only those which we need to simulate for manual testing of an SAP Greenfield implementation (or validate in case we can automate some testing already) and run all of them in a matter of seconds without having any access to the 3rd party systems or EDI partner landscapes. 

 

Figure 1 - simulating EDI partners from the copilot using Int4 Suite  

Key Benefits of Using AI Copilot in Greenfield SAP S/4HANA Transformations

Effortless Historical Data Access:
With a Copilot integrated into the SAP environment, historical messages, which serve as key test cases, can be loaded into SAP's vector database. We can use Int4 Robotic Crawler to download millions of historical messages as shown in Video 1. 

Video 1 - loading historical electronic messages to the SAP HANA Vector Engine 

This allows users to retrieve relevant test messages in real-time using simple, natural language queries, such as “Show test messages for vendor A” or “Find previous EDI transactions.” By searching in plain language, SAP developers and functional consultants save valuable time compared to traditional methods.

Boosted Simulation Capabilities:
Service virtualization, enhanced with AI Copilot functionality, facilitates faster simulation of third-party systems and EDI partners across various SAP S/4HANA tiers. By leveraging historical messages and simulation frameworks, project teams can operate at a pace significantly faster than before, especially when compared to manually requesting or recreating each test scenario individually. Copilot also speaks all languages as per Figure 2. 

Figure 2 - Simulations and validations work in all languages 

Overcoming Common Greenfield Transformation Challenges

Inaccessible Third-Party Systems and EDI Partners:
Many third-party systems and EDI partners may be unavailable or not yet configured for SAP Greenfield projects, causing delays. The AI Copilot, paired with service virtualization, provides a dynamic workaround by simulating these unavailable systems instantly and at scale, letting projects move forward without depending on external readiness. Copilot would resent historical electronic messages simulating the 3rd party systems and EDI partners as shown in Figure 3.

Figure 3 - Copilot can either only simulate messages going to SAP S/4HANA or also validate them. 

Reducing Manual Mockups:

Traditionally, mockup creation is a time-intensive task, especially for outbound messages like IDOCs or ABAP proxies. The Copilot automates much of this by retrieving historical message templates or generating new, reusable ones, eliminating delays caused by manual mockup processes.

The Role of AI Copilot in SAP S/4HANA Transformation Programs

An AI Copilot enhances the effectiveness of service virtualization in several ways:

  • SAP Functional Consultants:
    By streamlining the search and retrieval of test messages within SAP, consultants can quickly simulate and validate business processes directly in the system without waiting on external systems to be ready, keeping projects on schedule.

  • ABAP and Interface Developers:
    Copilot enables the seamless capture and reuse of messages from other environments, whether production or test, for use in development. This automation expedites the interface testing process by providing instant access to relevant data from other landscapes.

  • Project and Release Managers:
    Copilot assists with test cycle coordination by managing and simulating multiple third-party systems, reducing the number of dependent applications and allowing project teams to focus on core SAP functionalities.

  • Testing Teams:
    AI Copilot helps teams zero in on SAP S/4HANA testing by handling simulations for third-party components. This minimizes the need for extensive, manual third-party tests and allows testing to proceed smoothly.

Leveraging Copilot-Driven Simulation for Cost and Time Efficiency

Reduced Testing Time

Using Copilot to guide simulations during DEV and SIT phases results in faster testing cycles, as third-party system dependencies are alleviated through swift, AI-facilitated simulations.

Significant Cost Savings

By simulating the landscape of non-SAP systems through Copilot, SAP S/4HANA projects can significantly cut down on costs associated with setting up, configuring, and maintaining various systems across multiple testing environments. These savings contribute not only to the bottom line but also to project sustainability.

Early Defect Detection

Copilot supports extensive test runs by readily providing relevant test cases, enabling early identification of potential issues, which reduces production costs and minimizes the risks of operational disruptions later on.

Optimizing SAP S/4HANA Transformations with Historical Data in a Vector Database

Historical data and message logs, stored in an SAP S/4HANA vector database, become powerful assets in testing. By loading these past messages, SAP teams can directly access previous transactions and replicate them as needed through the Copilot. This approach eliminates redundant testing steps and delivers tailored test cases on demand, ensuring quicker response times and fewer bottlenecks.

Maximizing Simulation Impact in Greenfield SAP S/4HANA Projects

While simulations are traditionally beneficial for regression testing during UAT, the introduction of Copilot-driven simulations shines most during early stages like DEV and SIT. By implementing Copilot-assisted simulations early, project teams bypass the challenges posed by unavailable third-party systems, achieve higher test quality, and accelerate the Greenfield SAP S/4HANA transformation process.

Learning More About Copilot and Service Virtualization for SAP S/4HANA Transformations

Explore the synergy of AI Copilot and service virtualization with in-depth resources like the OpenSAP/SAP Learning course, “Avoid SAP S/4HANA Project Delays with Third-Party Systems Service Virtualization.” This course covers the fundamentals of service virtualization, its optimal use cases, and how it integrates with tools like Copilot to boost SAP S/4HANA project efficiency while minimizing environmental impact.
